common_name: New Guinea Impatiens (Busy Lizzie, Star Impatiens)

planting.note: Plant transplants after last frost in partial to full shade; in zone 9, New Guinea Impatiens performs best March through November when planted in morning sun or dappled shade with afternoon protection from the intense summer sun. Unlike common Impatiens (I. walleriana), it tolerates more direct morning sun and is less susceptible to Impatiens downy mildew. Pinch stem tips at planting to encourage bushy growth and prolific flowering. In zone 9b, it sometimes persists into winter in sheltered spots.

directions: Plant in morning sun to partial shade with consistently moist, well-drained, organically rich soil; New Guinea Impatiens requires more light than common impatiens and will become leggy and bloom poorly in deep shade, but performs best with protection from the harsh afternoon sun of zone 9 summers. Impatiens hawkeri is a species native to Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands that served as the primary genetic source for the large-flowered New Guinea Impatiens hybrids that have become among the most popular shade-tolerant annual flowers in American horticulture since their introduction in the 1970s. The flowers are significantly larger (2–3 inches) and the foliage more diverse than common I. walleriana — leaves may be dark green, bronze, variegated cream-and-green, or deeply colored — and many modern hybrids carry contrasting leaf and flower colors simultaneously. New Guinea Impatiens are critically important for gardeners as a replacement for the traditional common impatiens (I. walleriana) following the widespread outbreak of Impatiens Downy Mildew (Plasmopara obducens) beginning around 2010, which devastated I. walleriana plantings across the US. New Guinea Impatiens are resistant to the mildew and have largely taken the place of common impatiens in commercial and home landscapes. Flowers come in every color except true blue: white, red, orange, pink, salmon, coral, lavender, and bicolors.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Impatiens species for Florida landscapes
Clemson Cooperative Extension — New Guinea Impatiens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Annuals for Shade: New Guinea Impatiens
American Phytopathological Society — Impatiens Downy Mildew (Plasmopara obducens)

common_name: Coral Berry Ardisia (Christmas Berry, Hen's Eyes, Marlberry)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in partial to deep shade under established trees; Ardisia crenata thrives in the dry, root-competitive shade beneath mature live oaks and other large trees where most ornamentals fail. In zone 9a, plants may freeze to the ground in hard winters but resprout from the crown in spring. INVASIVE NOTE: Ardisia crenata is listed as invasive in Florida, Texas, and other Gulf Coast states where birds disperse seeds into natural areas — do not plant near forests, wetlands, or parks. Legally restricted in some counties.

directions: Plant in partial to deep shade in any well-drained soil — Coral Berry Ardisia is one of the most shade- and drought-tolerant small shrubs available, performing in conditions of deep dry shade under large oaks that defeat nearly all other ornamentals. Ardisia crenata (also listed as A. crispa) is a small, upright evergreen shrub from India, Southeast Asia, and Japan in the Primulaceae family that produces one of the most striking winter ornamental displays in zone 9 gardens: during November–February, pendulous clusters of bright coral-red to vivid scarlet round berries the size of small pearls hang in dramatic arching sprays below the dark, glossy, wavy-edged, evergreen leaves. The berries persist on the plant for months, and fully loaded plants in December–January are extraordinarily ornamental. The plant blooms in summer with small, inconspicuous white to pinkish flowers that are followed by the developing berries. White-berried ('Alba') and pink-berried forms are available. CAUTION: Classified as invasive in Florida, Texas, Louisiana, Georgia, and other Gulf and Southeast states — birds devour the berries and disperse seeds freely into natural areas where the plant displaces native understory species. Remove berries before they fall if planting in areas with access to natural lands.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ardisia crenata invasive plant assessment
Texas Invasives — Ardisia crenata (Coral Berry) profile
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade Shrubs for Louisiana Landscapes
USDA Plants Database — Ardisia crenata

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring or fall in partial to full shade; Sweet Box establishes readily and is low-maintenance once settled. It tolerates both dry shade and moderately moist conditions. In zone 9, it performs as an evergreen shrub blooming December–February; the tiny white flowers are barely visible but produce an intense, sweet vanilla-honey fragrance detectable from 10–15 feet away on mild winter days. Hardy to about 0°F when established.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist to dry, well-drained soil; Sweet Box is exceptionally tolerant of dry shade and root competition, making it one of the best low-growing shade shrubs for planting beneath established trees. Sarcococca confusa is a low, spreading, slowly suckering evergreen shrub in the boxwood family (Buxaceae) native to China, notable for producing its tiny but extraordinarily fragrant flowers in the dead of winter — December through February in zone 7–9 — when little else in the garden is blooming. The individual white flowers are barely noticeable: small tufts of stamens without petals, hidden among the glossy, lance-shaped, dark-green leaves. But the fragrance they produce is intensely sweet and reminiscent of vanilla and honey, and on mild winter days it carries remarkably far from a planting, making Sweet Box one of the most valuable fragrant plants for the shade garden. After flowering, small black or red berry-like fruits provide additional ornamental interest and are eaten by birds. The plant spreads gradually by underground suckers to form a dense, ground-covering colony 2–3 feet high and 4–6 feet across over many years. Essentially pest- and disease-free, drought-tolerant in shade, and long-lived, it is among the finest low-maintenance shade shrubs available.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Sarcococca confusa (AGM plant)
North Carolina State Extension Horticulture — Sarcococca species
Dirr, M.A. (2009) Manual of Woody Landscape Plants — Sarcococca confusa
University of Georgia Extension — Fragrant Winter Shrubs for the Southeast

planting.note: Propagate exclusively from stem cuttings (6–12 inch hardwood cuttings root readily in moist soil at 70°F+); true seeds are uncommon in cultivation. Plant rooted cuttings after last frost in full sun to partial shade. In zone 9, Chaya freezes to the ground in hard winters but resprouts vigorously from the root crown in spring. Mulch heavily. The stinging hairs on young leaves and stems cause skin irritation — wear gloves when harvesting. IMPORTANT: all parts of the plant are toxic if eaten raw — cooking for at least 5 minutes destroys the hydrocyanic glycosides and makes the leaves safe and nutritious.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil; Chaya is extremely drought-tolerant once established and adapts to poor, sandy, or clay soils with minimal fertilizer. Cnidoscolus chayamansa is a spinach tree native to the Yucatan Peninsula and Central America where it has been cultivated as a staple cooked green by the Maya civilization for over a millennium. The leaves are exceptionally nutritious — with higher concentrations of protein, calcium, iron, vitamin C, and beta-carotene than most leafy vegetables including kale and spinach — and Chaya has become a widely promoted food security crop in tropical and subtropical regions worldwide. The plant is an incredibly productive cut-and-come-again perennial: harvest the top 6–10 inches of stem tips continuously through the warm season and the plant regrows rapidly. The large, dark-green, deeply-lobed, maple-like leaves are strikingly ornamental. CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE: Never eat Chaya raw — the fresh leaves contain hydrocyanic glycosides that can cause poisoning. Cook for a minimum of 5 minutes (boiling, steaming, or sautéing) to neutralize the toxins. Once cooked, the greens are safe and delicious, tasting like a mild spinach-collard cross. In zone 9, Chaya is one of the most productive warm-season leafy greens available, producing from May through November even in summer heat that wilts conventional spinach.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Cnidoscolus chayamansa nutritional composition
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Chaya (Tree Spinach) for Texas Gardens
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Chaya (Cnidoscolus chayamansa)
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Edibles for Gulf Coast Louisiana: Chaya

common_name: Giant Upright Elephant Ear (Night-Scented Lily, Asian Taro)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in spring when soil temperature reaches 65°F+ in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil. Unlike most Colocasia elephant ears which prefer full sun and wet conditions, Alocasia odora thrives in partial to deep shade with moist but well-drained soil. In zone 9, the rhizomes survive mild winters; mulch heavily in zone 9a. Divide clumps every 3–4 years. The fragrant spathes appear in spring and early summer.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Alocasia odora is distinctly more shade-tolerant than most Colocasia elephant ears and performs best in the dappled light of woodland gardens, shaded borders, and understory plantings where its massive, upright, arching leaves create a dramatic tropical effect. Alocasia odora is a clumping tropical perennial from China, Japan, and Southeast Asia with bold, arrow-shaped leaves that can reach 3–4 feet long on upright petioles 3–5 feet tall — forming a bold, architecturally dramatic mass in the shade garden that evokes a tropical jungle. Unlike Colocasia whose leaf stems attach at the center of the leaf (peltate), Alocasia leaves attach at the notch — making it easy to tell the two genera apart. The common name 'Night-Scented Lily' refers to the fragrant greenish-white spathes (similar to calla lily flowers) produced in spring, which release a sweet, vanilla-like fragrance after dark. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens, Alocasia odora is one of the finest large foliage plants for bringing tropical scale to shaded spots — under live oaks, in north-facing beds, or along shaded house walls — where conventional sun-loving plants would fail. In mild winters it remains evergreen; a hard freeze browns the foliage but the underground rhizomes survive to resprout vigorously.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alocasia species for Florida landscapes
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Foliage for Shaded Louisiana Gardens: Alocasia
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Alocasia odora
Royal Horticultural Society — Alocasia odora cultivation notes
